discussion_posts.name: Frind
discussion_posts.content: bad problem, struggled with the definition, what is "largest \nsubtree" exactly ?

discussion_posts.name: WorldHello
discussion_posts.content: The first test case - [10,5,15,1,8,null,7] has wrong solution. The answer should not be 3. The largest BST subtree is - the one with nodes - 10, 5, 1, 8, 15. The answer should be 5 nodes.\n\n

discussion_posts.name: user4202S
discussion_posts.content: description of BST is not correct

discussion_posts.name: alenny
discussion_posts.content: The test cases do not treat a subtree with duplicate values as a BST. But in the Wiki definition, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Binary_search_tree, BST allows duplicate values.

discussion_posts.name: tin_le
discussion_posts.content: Hint : have a tuple [count, minVal, maxVal]

discussion_posts.name: andvalsol
discussion_posts.content: Why does the answer for the second test case is 2 and not 3? Since the subtree is 4 -> 7 -> 2

discussion_posts.name: Hekillno
discussion_posts.content: emm, if "the largest means subtree has the largest number of nodes.", then isn\'t the larget subtree the whole tree itself?

discussion_posts.name: jkveda
discussion_posts.content: Why is [1,2] considered a valid BST and the answer is 1?

discussion_posts.name: cswartzell
discussion_posts.content: What an awful problem. Not a BST, and expects you to return the *NUMBER* of nodes of said "BST", even though that isnt what the problem asks for: it says "find the largest subtree", and doesn\'t give any instructions on what to return. The natural assumption would be to return the node that is the root of this BST. How has this problem stood this long without correction?
